title: Software Licensing Types
obj: Students will be able to identify software license types, explain the rights and restrictions of each, and select the appropriate license for a given organizational need.
content: vendor retains ownership; EULA restrictions; per-seat, per-device, subscription models
GPL (copyleft), MIT/BSD (permissive), Apache; what each allows users to do and redistribute
free to use but closed-source; shareware trial limitations; donationware
perpetual vs. subscription; OEM vs. retail; volume licensing (Microsoft EA, VLA)
audit risk; true-up; software asset management (SAM); consequences of non-compliance
CC licenses for content (not software); BY, SA, ND, NC variants
assess: License sort: classify 10 software products by license type using their official license text
Scenario: a school wants to deploy an app on 500 machines — which license model is most cost-effective?
Short answer: what is the key difference between GPL and MIT open-source licenses?

title: Programming Language Categories
obj: Students will be able to classify programming languages by type and abstraction level, and match each category to its common use cases in IT work.
content: machine code and assembly; directly control hardware; used in firmware, drivers, embedded systems
C, C++, Rust, Go; compiled to machine code; fast execution; used in OS and systems programming
Python, JavaScript, Ruby, Bash; run via interpreter; rapid development; used in automation and web
Java, C#; compiled to bytecode then JIT-compiled; platform-independent; enterprise use
SQL for databases; not general-purpose; declarative style
HTML, CSS, XML, JSON; not Turing-complete; structure and presentation of data
assess: Language classification: sort 12 languages into the correct categories
Use-case matching: match 5 IT tasks (automate file backup, build a web page, query a database, write a device driver, create an Android app) to the best language choice
Short answer: why would a DevOps engineer choose Python over C++ for an automation script?

title: Algorithms, Flowcharts, and Control Flow
obj: Students will be able to define an algorithm, read and draw a flowchart, and trace the execution of common control-flow structures.
content: a finite, ordered set of steps to solve a problem; correctness, efficiency, termination
oval (start/end), rectangle (process), diamond (decision), parallelogram (I/O); drawing conventions
statements execute top to bottom; most basic control flow
if/else, switch/case; decision branching; truth tables for and/or/not
while, for, do-while loops; loop counter, condition, termination; infinite loop risk
language-agnostic algorithm description; bridge between English and code
assess: Flowchart reading: trace through a provided flowchart with specific input values and state the output
Flowchart drawing: draw a flowchart for the algorithm 'find the largest number in a list of 5 numbers'
Short answer: what makes an algorithm more efficient — fewer steps or faster steps?

title: Programming Concepts (Identifiers, Functions, Objects)
obj: Students will be able to define core programming vocabulary including variables, functions, objects, and libraries, and recognize these constructs in sample code.
content: naming rules, data types in code, scope (local vs. global), constants
defining and calling functions; parameters and return values; reusability principle
class as a blueprint; object as an instance; properties and methods; encapsulation concept
importing pre-written code; standard library vs. third-party packages; API calls
exceptions and try/catch; defensive coding; logging errors for troubleshooting
recognizing Python, JavaScript, and Bash snippets; understanding enough to maintain scripts
assess: Code reading: given a 15-line Python script, identify three variables, one function, and one loop
Vocabulary match: match 10 programming terms to definitions without notes
Short answer: why do programmers use functions instead of copying the same code multiple times?

title: Database Concepts and Purpose
obj: Students will be able to explain why databases are used, describe the role of a DBMS, and identify common database use cases in business and IT contexts.
content: structured, persistent, multi-user data storage; advantages over flat files and spreadsheets
software layer between data and users; enforces rules, handles concurrency, manages storage
MySQL, PostgreSQL, Microsoft SQL Server, Oracle, SQLite; open-source vs. commercial
ticketing systems, Active Directory, asset management, inventory — all use databases behind the scenes
raw data transformed into meaningful information via queries and reports
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — why transactions must be reliable
assess: Use-case identification: for 6 business scenarios, explain why a database is better than a spreadsheet
Short answer: what does it mean for a database to be ACID-compliant and why does it matter?
Discussion: name two databases an IT support technician indirectly uses every day

title: Database Structures (Relational and Non-Relational)
obj: Students will be able to describe the structure of relational databases using tables, keys, and relationships, and compare relational to non-relational database types.
content: relation = table; tuple = row; attribute = column; every row is unique (primary key)
primary key uniquely identifies a row; foreign key links rows across tables; referential integrity
one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many; junction tables; ER diagram basics
1NF/2NF/3NF — eliminating redundancy; why normalized data is easier to maintain
relational for structured, transactional data; NoSQL for scale, flexibility, unstructured data
assess: ER diagram: given a scenario (school with students, courses, and grades), draw the entities and relationships
Key identification: given a sample table, identify the primary key and any foreign keys
Short answer: what problem does normalization solve?

title: Database Interfaces and Query Languages
obj: Students will be able to write basic SQL queries to retrieve, filter, and sort data, and describe how non-technical users interact with databases through application interfaces.
content: SELECT columns FROM table; * wildcard; aliases; running queries in a client tool
comparison operators, AND/OR/NOT, LIKE for pattern matching, NULL handling
ORDER BY ASC/DESC; LIMIT/TOP; why result order isn't guaranteed without ORDER BY
COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX; GROUP BY; HAVING to filter groups
INNER JOIN to combine related tables; concept of LEFT JOIN; reading a query with two tables
forms, reports, dashboards as front-ends to databases; why end users rarely write SQL
assess: SQL lab: run five provided queries against a sample database and record the results
Write a query: given a table schema, write a query to find all employees in a specific department sorted by salary
Short answer: what is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?

title: Virtualization Concepts
obj: Students will be able to explain virtualization, distinguish between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors, and describe common IT uses for virtual machines.
content: abstracting hardware to run multiple OS instances on one physical host; key benefits (consolidation, isolation)
bare-metal; runs directly on hardware; VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM; used in data centers
runs on a host OS; VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels; used for desktops and testing
vCPU, vRAM, virtual disk (VMDK/VHD), virtual NIC; how each maps to physical resources
snapshot saves VM state; clone creates a full copy; use in testing and rollback scenarios
overcommitting RAM/CPU; impact on VM performance; why VMs slow each other down
assess: Hypervisor comparison: create a table comparing Type 1 vs. Type 2 on 5 criteria
Short answer: why would a school IT lab use VMs instead of physical machines for student practice?
Scenario: a technician wants to safely test malware — what VM configuration minimizes risk to the host?
